item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Hopefully the corpse can finally "rest in peace" now that the gun is in containment and (probably) won't be used anymore.
+1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: The Grahams and the Tewksburys were real families that participated in the Pleasant Valley War in the late 1800s. That said, when I was coming up with a first name, someone suggested Joshua specifically as a reference to Fallout. I thought it was a fun little easter egg, so I kept it in.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: No, it's not.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Is this reference to fallout new veges cause I searched up the name "Joshua Graham", and this came up : https://fallout.fandom.com/wiki/Joshua_Graham

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: So hard to find an image of a Remington Model 1875 with ivory grips that satisfies the image policy. It shouldn't be this hard but it is.

item.title: Audio Adaptation
item.content:encoded:
I appreciate the motif: an old west land feud is classic Americana.

Man, even our classic history includes people shooting each other. *Shots Fired* *Literally*

Anyways, here's the reading: https://youtu.be/wzkSSjc7X5Y

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: That'd be either the world's lamest or the coolest Visions of a Better World entry.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: It occurs to me that this is usuable as an unjammable infinite-range one-way communicator.

item.title: Re: What?
item.content:encoded:
This SCP has, like, one paragraph, and yet still remains.

Length in itself is not grounds for deletion. So long as the work is finished, it's not subject to summary deletion. Also, as Deci stated, this was an entry in the Short Works Contest. Note the sc2015 tag.

How long has this SCP been on this site?

You can check article age by clicking the "History" link at the bottom of a page, and looking for the page creation date.

I get 8 hours, and mine is already on the verge of immediate deletion.

As per the Deletions Guide, all articles not subject to summary deletion (for being unfinished work, trolling, or spam) are given a 24-hour grace period. If you notice staff incorrectly implementing this, please notify a Team Captain and let them know that the deletions process was carried out incorrectly.

Not that I'm surprised, but still… Really?

…yes? If you're confused as to why something is rated the way it is, consider reading through the other comments in this discussion forum and responding to them.

item.title: Re: What?
item.content:encoded: SCP-173 is only 15 sentences. Nothing wrong with a short article as long as it delivers.

item.title: Re: What?
item.content:encoded: A couple months. This SCP was created for the Short Works contest, and was written by someone who has experienxe in SCP writing. Very short SCPs can suceed if you know what you're doing.

item.title: What?
item.content:encoded:
Wha- is there something I'm missing?
This SCP has, like, one paragraph, and yet still remains. How long has this SCP been on this site?
I get 8 hours, and mine is already on the verge of immediate deletion. Not that I'm surprised, but still…
Really?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Joshua Graham was already mentioned. So is this a different Joshua Graham?

How did he not drop the revolver when he died? Why was he holding it when he died?

I'm assuming Joshua Graham wanted to kill Stanley Tewksbury, why didn't he?

Maybe I'm just dumb — but I just don't get it. -1.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: One of my favorite contest entries, although I agree with others that it looses some mystery by tying it to a real event.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
WOW. Efficiency at its best!

Not too long, not too short. Perfect. Mainlist or not, contest or not. The IRL feud doesn't change much for me: it's still a good piece of writing, serving a simple yet thought-provoking story.

Upvote, with cheers.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Huh. I really hadn't thought about the reloading part.

As for the rest, I got the "Old West land feud" thing right away, which is why I upvoted.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
This is the kind of entry I was hoping for when the contest was announced. Short, punchy, evocative of the good attributes of the older style of articles. The line at the end did a really good job of engaging my interest and providing a nice "wham!" moment.

Unfortunately, tying it to a real-life historical event really took the sense of mystery out of it for me. Before I read that, my imagination was going in all sorts of places, wondering what could possibly explain this mummified dude's hand pumping bullets, reloading, and pumping more bullets into this other guy. That's just enough info to feed the reader to really get their curiosity going. But now, the context is "long-delayed vendetta over an Old West land dispute". It feels kind of deflating.

I can't upvote with that feeling now hanging over the article, but for all of the positive aspects I outlined above, I can't downvote either.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Second this. Also, if "pass through" means it doesn't penetrate objects, the anomalous effect is even less interesting.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I feel similarly.

item.title: Re: In the event that you can't figure it out...
item.content:encoded: And now I have my connection, and now you have your upvote. :D

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Well, it did say "pass through", not "penetrate". If that was what was meant, though, some note of "without damage" should have been made.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Wouldn't they keep the body as close as possible to the gun, in order to minimize damage to the facility?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I… don't feel anything from this. To me, that's kind of worse than outright not liking it; a lot of SCPs on the site make me feel something, whether it's contempt for the gimmick used in it, or enjoyment, or intrigue, or the urge to punch the researchers who logged the SCP in the face for being such genre-blind clods.

It's too short for me to feel anything. Honestly, part of me wonders if this wouldn't be downvoted to crap if it wasn't posted during the short contest. For my part, I don't feel anything for it, and neutrality towards a piece is ultimately a nail in the coffin for me.

Downvoted.

item.title: Re: In the event that you can't figure it out...
item.content:encoded:
Oh, cool. IRL history. I always love those.
Easy +1 on my part.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Liked in chat, like it here. I was hoping for more stuff of this kind when the contest open.

item.title: Re: In the event that you can't figure it out...
item.content:encoded: Pretty close.

item.title: In the event that you can't figure it out...
item.content:encoded:
Spoilers
– hide block

These two ranchers had a feud, and the one whose mummified corpse is holding the gun probably vowed to kill the other one. Somehow, that vow has held true even after nearly a century, and even though the vow came true too late.

The author can correct me if I'm wrong, but I think this fits the minimalist nature of the contest well.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Pros:

  • There's a faint hint of a story behind these objects, and I like that despite not being really specified here.

Cons:

  • The only complaint I got was that it will take a reader a while to know what is SCP-2158-1 and subsequently figure out what's going on in general.

Verdict: Neutral vote

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I've got nothing against short articles, especially considering the contest right now, but right now all I see here is a really, really cool anomalous item list entry. It needs more substance to push it from an interesting but ultimately simple oddity to a true SCP that warrants special documentation.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Okay, is Joshua Graham you making a Fallout reference, or did you just happen upon that name?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: This is compelling, but it needs something more. I find myself wondering why, what's the connection between these people. Also, "held in the hand of" is kind of a nonspecific way of putting it; mentioning that it's attached to a mummified hand would be a better way to go.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: You can freely edit up until the posting period ends at midnight (PDT) tonight.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Lock expired, reverted edit in case it skirted the edge of the rules regarding contest entries.

The missing "a" is at the beginning of the description, after the word "is". I only meant to make a minor grammar edit, but decided it was better to err on the site of allowing the author complete control over the page, since the contest is still going on.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Sorry, edited in a missing "a" without realizing it was a contest entry. Went to revert, since I couldn't immediately recall whether minor grammar editing by other people was allowed (I since checked, the contest page doesn't say anything definitive either way), but page was locked by author.

My apologies.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Agreed. I like it, and I want to upvote it, but it feels like just a little more would help it out a lot. It reminds me of Aelanna's suspending bullet; just enough more context would help it turn from "alright" to "really effective". You still have another 9 hours before major edits are closed off anyway.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Short, simple, effective. It comes in at 163 words. Sadly, I feel like you lost an opportunity here to embellish a bit more. 73 more words of context would have still kept you under the mark for that bonus.
